coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction performance of flower-like CoHS @NCDs through in-situ coupling of Nitrogen-Doped carbon dots and cobalt hydroxide nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yang
Qi, Haiyan
Jing, Tao
Li, Jun
Shen, Siqi
Zeng, Qingxin
Zhao, Hongxu
coredata.dc:description: Efficient electrocatalytic materials are crucial for enhancing the efficiency of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in hydrolysis applications. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) via a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, 3D flower-shaped CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts were prepared by the integration of NCDs and cobalt hydroxide nanosheets (CoHS) using a solvothermal method. The resulting CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts demonstrated outstanding catalytic performance for OER under alkaline conditions. Specifically, the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst exhibited an overpotential of 280 mV at 10 mA cm−2(314 mV at 50 mA cm−2), representing an 80 mV improvement compared to CoHS, along with remarkable durability. Through comprehensive characterization, we revealed that the incorporation of NCDs facilitated the creation of numerous exposed active sites on CoHS, forming a unique electron transfer pathway (Co-N), thereby optimizing the kinetics of the OER reaction. This study underscored the significant potential of constructing electrocatalysts with exceptional OER activity based on transition metal hydroxides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxygen evolution reaction electrocatalysts
Cobalt hydroxide nanosheets

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (145309525); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart representation of NCDs synthesis and preparation of CoHS electrocatalysts with diverse morphologies.
(a) HRTEM image, (b) Size distribution diagram, and (c) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs; (d) PL spectra of NCDs, CoHS, and CoHS@NCDs-3.
SEM images of (a) CoHS, (b) CoHS@NCDs-1, (c) CoHS@NCDs-2, (d) CoHS@NCDs-3, (e) CoHS@NCDs-4, (f) CoHS@NCDs-5, and (g) CoHS@NCDs-6.

SEM image of (a) CoHS and (b) CoHS@NCDs-3; TEM image of (c) CoHS and (d) CoHS@NCDs-3; (e) HRTEM image of CoHS@NCDs-3; (f) Corresponding EDS mapping with Co (purple), O(yellow), C (red), and N (green) of CoHS@NCDs-3.
XRD pattern (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of NCDs, CoHS, and CoHS@NCDs-3.
(a) Full-scan XPS spectra of CoHS, CoHS@NCDs-3 and NCDs; (b) High-resolution (b)C1s, (c)O1s, and (d)N1s of NCDs, CoHS and CoHS@NCDs-3; (e) Nitrogen content percentage of NCDs, CoHS, and CoHS@NCDs-3.
OER performance electrocatalysts. (a) LSV curves in 1.0 M KOH; (b) Overpotential bar graph; (c) Tafel plots; (d) Plots applied to calculate the Cdl.
Nyquist plots of CoHS and CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts.
(a) Long term stability test of CoHS@NCDs-3 at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2 for 100 h; (b) OER LSV curves of CoHS@NCDs-3 before (red) and after (black) undergoing 1000 CV cycles.
Cdl and ECSA data for CoHS and CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction performance of flower-like CoHS @NCDs through in-situ coupling of Nitrogen-Doped carbon dots and cobalt hydroxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp, State Administration for Market Regulation, Qiqihar, Heilongjiang 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp, State Administration for Market Regulation, Qiqihar, Heilongjiang 161006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient electrocatalytic materials are crucial for enhancing the efficiency of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in hydrolysis applications. In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) via a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, 3D flower-shaped CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts were prepared by the integration of NCDs and cobalt hydroxide nanosheets (CoHS) using a solvothermal method. The resulting CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts demonstrated outstanding catalytic performance for OER under alkaline conditions. Specifically, the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst exhibited an overpotential of 280 mV at 10 mA cm−2(314 mV at 50 mA cm−2), representing an 80 mV improvement compared to CoHS, along with remarkable durability. Through comprehensive characterization, we revealed that the incorporation of NCDs facilitated the creation of numerous exposed active sites on CoHS, forming a unique electron transfer pathway (Co-N), thereby optimizing the kinetics of the OER reaction. This study underscored the significant potential of constructing electrocatalysts with exceptional OER activity based on transition metal hydroxides.

ce:section.ce:para: Renewable energy sources, derived from solar radiation, wind, water, biomass, and geothermal heat, offer a sustainable alternative to conventional energy sources. They emit minimal greenhouse gases and pollutants into the atmosphere, making them crucial in combating climate change and achieving environmental sustainability [1]. As global population grows and industrial activities expand, the demand for renewable energy continues to escalate [2,3]. Hydrogen (H2) has emerged as a promising clean energy vector through its high energy consumption and widespread availability [4,5]. Industrial hydrogen production primarily involves three methods: carbon monoxide (CO) extraction from fossil fuels followed by water gas shift reaction, methanol reforming, and water electrolysis. Electrolysis, particularly, stands out for its simplicity, low energy consumption, minimal greenhouse gas emissions, high hydrogen purity, and low impurity content [6–9]. However, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) within the electrolysis process suffers from slow kinetics, necessitating substantial overpotential [10,11].

Traditionally, precious metal-based electrocatalysts, for instance iridium (Ir) and ruthenium (Ru) and their oxides, have shown promise in enhancing OER efficiency. Nonetheless, their scarcity and high costs pose significant limitations [12–16]. Consequently, research has focused on developing non-precious transition metal-based catalysts [17–20], including oxides, hydroxides, sulfides [21], selenides [22], phosphates [23], carbides [24], borides [25], and nitrides [26]. Among these, two-dimensional (2D) OER electrocatalysts, particularly layered hydroxides (LDHs), have garnered significant attention for their abundant catalytic active sites, high surface area ratio, adjustable chemical compositions, porous structures, strong electrostatic interactions between interlayer anions and excellent stability [27–29]. Nevertheless, LDHs suffer from poor conductivity, corrosion susceptibility, and insufficient active sites [30].

To address these limitations, electrocatalysts incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been explored. CQDs has abundant functional groups, surface chemical properties, electronic characteristics [31]. Multi-component doping with heteroatoms may provide more favorable binding active sites of the preparation of high-performance composite materials, promoting electron transfer within the composite materials, thereby improving electrocatalytic performance and corrosion resistance [32,33]. The electronic structure of nitrogen atom is similar to that of carbon atom, and nitrogen atom can contribute its unpaired electrons as electron donors, which can effectively improve the reaction between carbon structures and external molecules. Therefore, N-doped CQDs can enhance catalytic activity. The introduction of nitrogen atom into CQDs may form new metal-N (Me-N) active sites, effectively improving OER. In addition, the negative charge of CQDs will attract metal cations and control crystal growth. In summary, nitrogen doped carbon dots have great potential for improving electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reactions.

In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using a one-step hydrothermal method, followed by the preparation of 3D nano flower-shaped CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts via a solvothermal method. Our results demonstrated that the incorporation of NCDs facilitated the exposure of active sites on cobalt hydroxide nanosheets (CoHS), establishing a unique electron transfer pathway (Co-N), thereby optimizing OER kinetics. The resulting CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts exhibited a flower-like structure with a significantly enlarged electrochemical active surface area, leading to an 80 mV improvement in performance at equivalent current densities.

Citric acid (99.5 %), ethylenediamine (AR grade), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (99.5 %), 2-methylimidazole (98 %), and potassium hydroxide (95 %) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (CP grade) was obtained from, Beijing Chemical Plant. Acetone (99.5 %) and methanol (99.5 %) were acquired from Liaoning Quanrui Reagent Co., Ltd (Liaoning, China). Nickel foam (NF) was procured from Suzhou Taili foam Metal Factory (Jiangsu, China). The dialysis membrane was acquired from Beijing Jinghua Tongtianrui Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Deionized (DI) water from a Beijing Puxi GMB-2 ultrapure water preparation system (Beijing, China) was utilized for all experimentations.

NCDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method following established procedures. Briefly, 11.4 g of citric acid was dissolved in a mixed solution consisting of 120 mL of DI water and 4.02 mL of ethylenediamine. After ultrasonic agitation, we transferred the solution to a 30 ml high-pressure autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The resulting dark brown product was dialyzed with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 500 kDa for 48 h and subsequently freeze-dried to fetch NCDs in a powdered form, which were stored for future applications.

Cobalt hydroxide nanosheets (CoHS) were prepared via a solvent thermal method. Briefly, 2.91 g of Co(NO3)2̇6H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of methanol to prepare solution A, while 0.082 g of 2-methylimidazole was added to in 10 mL of methanol to form solution B. Solution A and solution B were mixed thoroughly to obtain solution C. Solution C, along with clean nickel foam (NF), were transferred into a 50 mL stainless steel high-pressure vessel lined with PTFE. The surface area of the nickel foam working electrode was 1 cm × 0.7 cm. The vessel was then heated at 140 °C for 12 h and allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature. The resulting sample was washed with DI water and methanol, followed by overnight drying at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven to yield the CoHS catalyst.

The synthesis of CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts followed an analogous procedure to CoHS preparation, with the incorporation of varying amounts of NCDs powder into the precursor mixture. Specifically, 1, 3, 5, 10, 20, and 30 mg of NCDs were added, corresponding to samples labeled as CoHS@NCDs-1 to CoHS@NCDs-6. Each mixture, along with 2 tablets of NF, was placed in a 30 mL stainless steel high-pressure vessel lined with PTFE and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, the NF was removed, and the resulting material was washed thrice with DI water and methanol. The washed samples were then dried overnight at 60 °C in an oven, yielding CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts for subsequent applications. The average mass loading of the catalyst was 0.0462 ± 0.0030 g/cm2.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with xenon lamps. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). The crystal structure was analyzed using a Bruker-D8-FOCUS X-ray diffraction system (Billerica, MA, USA) with Cu kɑ radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å), operating within a 2θ range of 0 to 90°. The microstructure and morphology of the electrocatalysts were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on JEOL JSM-7800F PRIME (Akishima, Tokyo, Japan) and Hitachi Regulus SU 8230 (Chiyoda City, Tokyo, Japan) systems. TEM and HRTEM images were acquired using the EMSIS Veleta G3 (Münster, Germany) and JEOL JEM-2100F (Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted using a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). Physicochemical analyses were conducted using powder samples obtained by centrifugal washing from the hydrothermal autoclave to minimize the influence of contaminants from NF.

All electrochemical measurements were conducted at ambient temperature in a 1 M KOH solution (pH = 13.9) using a CHI 660 E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China). A standard three-electrode system was employed, consisting of platinum sheets as counter electrodes, Ag/AgCl electrodes as reference electrodes, and NF as working electrodes. To standardize the measured potentials to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), the Nernst equation was applied: E RHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.059 × pH + 0.197. Priorto electrochemical characterizations, the system circulated 30 cycles at a scanning rate of 10 mV/s. Polarization data were acquired at the scanning rate of 5 mV/s by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with 95 % IR compensation. To evaluate cyclic durability, cyclic voltammetry method was applied, involving 1000 cycles at 10mVs−1. The current density (j) was calculate using the following formula j = A/S, where A was the current measured from the CV curve (mA), S was the geometric area of NF (0.07 cm2). Cdl was calculated based on the linear relationship: i = vCdl. Then the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was calculated by the following equation: ECSA = Cdl/Cs, where Cs was considered to be 0.04mF cm−2 in 1 M KOH solution. The EIS was executed at frequency from 105 to 0.1 Hz.

NCDs were produced by a bottom-up approach [34] and their morphology was examined via HRTEM. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2a) revealed uniformly dispersed spherical carbon particles, with an average size of approximately 2.1 nm (Fig. 2b). Excitation-dependent behavior was observed in NCDs, with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 nm and 445 nm, respectively (Fig. 2c). Additionally, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of NCDs exhibited significantly quenching upon interaction with CoHS (Fig. 2d), indicating efficient electron transfer between NCDs and CoHS.

The Zeta potential of the NCDs was measured (Fig. S1), and the measurement was −42.7 mV. The reason for the formation of different morphologies of CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts after adding different amounts of NCDs may be as follows [34]: in the precursor solution of cobalt hydroxide nanosheets, after adding NCDs, negative NCDs first undergo electrostatic interactions with cobalt cations through functional groups. As the temperature increases, cobalt cations combine with hydroxyl groups in NCDs to form cobalt hydroxide hydrate @NCDs nanoparticles. Then, the nanoparticles begin to grow and form nanosheets, with anions inserted between the layers. Due to the multifunctional groups and electrostatic interactions on the surface of NCDs, dozens of favorable sites on the nanosheets drive crystal growth. Excessive addition of NCDs leads to enhanced electrostatic interactions, resulting in agglomeration of nanosheets and the formation of spherical morphology.

SEM analysis indicated that both CoHS (Fig. 4 a) and the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst (Fig. 4b) exhibited a similar flower-like nanosheet structure. However, TEM analysis revealed uniform and densely packed small pores specifically on the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst nanosheets (Fig. 4c, d), likely due to the incorporation of NCDs. The HRTEM image of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst (Fig. 4e) demonstrated NCDs have been successfully doped into the CoHS, and an interplanar distance of 0.22 nm corresponding to the NCDs’ (100) crystal plane. And intraplanar distances of 0.23 nm, 0.23 nm, 0.15 nm, and 0.46 nm corresponding to the CoHS’ (101), (002), (003), and (001) crystal planes, respectively [34,35]. Furthermore, elemental mapping illustrated the uniform distribution of Co, O, C, and N on the surface of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst (Fig. 4f). These observations confirmed the prosperous synthesis of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst, demonstrating the presence of both CoHS and NCDs [36].

To validate the prosperous preparation of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst and the synergistic effect between CoHS and NCDs, further characterization analyses were performed. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 5 a) exhibited a broad peak at 22°, indicating a graphite like structure. Likewise, the XRD spectrum of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst displayed peaks at 19.0°, 36.8°, 44.7°, and 60.6°, attributed to the (001), (101), (002), and (003) planes of CoHS, respectively, confirming the presence of CoHS (Corresponding to PDF # 74–1057) [37]. Furthermore, an additional small peak at 22° in the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst aligned with the carbon atom peak at 22° in NCDs, confirming the successful doping of NCDs.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of NCDs revealed several characteristic peaks (Fig. 5b), including those at 3420 cm−1 and 2919 cm−1 associated with OH and CH bonds stretching vibrations, respectively. An absorption peak at 1697 cm−1 was accounted for the stretching vibration of CO bonds. Additionally, peaks at 1660 cm−1 and 1555 cm−1 indicated deformation vibrations of NH and CH bonds separately, while the peak at 1040 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C-NH-C bonds. This spectrum highlighted the presence of multiple functional groups in NCDs, facilitating their role as nucleation sites for electrocatalyst preparation [37]. Comparison of the FT-IR spectra of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst and CoHS revealed similarities, with the peak at 581 cm−1 corresponding to Co-O vibration [38]. In the electrocatalyst, a broadening peak observed at 1697 cm−1 suggested stretching vibration of CO bonds and bending vibration of NH bond. Conversely, the peak at 1381 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of CN bond, exhibited weaker intensity compared to CoHS. Furthermore, the diminished intensity of the peak at 3420 cm−1 indicated a strong interaction between CoHS and NCDs.

Elemental composition of CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst was researched by XPS, showing the existence of C, N, O, and Co (Fig. 6 a). In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 6b), NCDs, CoHS, and the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst exhibited additional peaks representing CC/CC, CN, and CO bonds [39]. Moreover, the peak of CoHS@NCDs-3 shifted to 283.5 eV compared with NCDs, with a significant decrease in CN content at 284.8 eV, indicating the coupling interaction between NCDs and CoHS [40]. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 6c) revealed distinct peaks associated with CO, OH, and CO bonds in NCDs, while CoHS exhibited peaks corresponding to Co-O and OH bonds. In the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst, peaks corresponding to Co-O bond (530.3 eV), OH groups (531.7 eV), and CO bonds (532.8 eV) were evident. Notably, the Co-O bond (531.1 eV) exhibited a peak shift compared to CoHS, along with the appearance of a new CO peak, further confirming the coupling interaction between NCDs and CoHS [41]. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 6d), the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst displayed peaks at 397.6 eV (Co-N bond), 399.9 eV (NH bond), and 402.1 eV (CN bond), compared with NCDs and CoHS, the peaks shift to the low field, indicative of the coupling effect between NCDs and CoHS. The nitrogen element in CoHS might be residual from cobalt nitrate hexahydrate. Compared with CoHS, the nitrogen content in CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst significantly increased to 8.49 % (Fig. 6e), further highlighting NCDs have been successfully doped into CoHS. In the Co2p spectrum (Fig. S2), the peak of CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst shifts towards a lower field compared to CoHS. Overall, the characterization of high-resolution elemental spectra in XPS revealed shifts or new peaks in CoHS@NCDs-3 compared to CoHS and NCDs, indicating the formation of Co-N resulted from the coupling of NCDs with CoHS. This coupling effect could enhance charge transfer and optimize the electronic structure, thereby potentially scaling up the electrochemical activity of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst. In addition, the surface area of our catalyst by BET analysis method, the results showed that the surface area of the catalyst got bigger with the increase of the NCDs content (Table S4).

Next, we assessed the catalytic properties of CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in 1 M KOH. The electrocatalysts were synthesized in situ on NF via a hydrothermal method and stabilized in the electrolyte using the CV method before conducting linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements. The LSV curves (Fig. 7 a) provided insights into the OER electrochemical performance of the CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts with varying NCDs amounts and RuO2. Notably, a reduction peak at ∼ 1.32 V (relative to RHE) was observed in both CoHS and most CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts, attributed to the Ni3+/Ni2+ or the Co 3+/Co2+ redox process in NF. The CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst, containing 5 mg of NCDs, demonstrated superior OER activity with an overpotential of 280 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and 314 mV at 50 mA cm−2, outperforming CoHS and other CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts (Fig. 7b). This suggested that the incorporation of an appropriate amount of NCDs enhanced the binding affinity of active sites, facilitating electron transfer and improving OER performance.

Additionally, the Tafel slope, a key parameter for evaluating OER kinetics, was determined from the LSV curve data (Fig. 7c). The CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst exhibited the fastest kinetics with a Tafel slope of 42.2 mV dec−1, lower than RuO2, CoHS and other CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts. Moreover, measurement of Cdl using the cyclic voltammetry method, and calculations of ECSA (Fig. S3 and Fig. 7d) revealed that the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst displayed the highest Cdl of 29.5 mF cm−2 and ECSA of 737.5 cm−2 compared to other electrocatalysts, indicating its efficient electrocatalytic active area for OER (Table 1 ). Combined with BET analysis, the results showed that it was also not a simple positive relative between ECSA and BET surface area. So we inferred that the catalytic activity of the catalyst was not directly related to the BET surface area. The total activity of catalyst is determined by intrinsic activity and amount of active sites. The increase of NCDs content in the catalyst could only raise amount of active sites to a certain extent, if the content of NCDs was too high, it might cause aggregation of material and decline the activity of catalyst, which was consistent with the results of morphology (Fig. 3). That could be another explanation for why the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst containing 5 mg of NCDs achieved the best activity.

To explore the interfacial charge transfer dynamics in electrochemical reactions, EIS were conducted (Fig. 8 ). Nyquist plots were obtained for all CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts. As expected, the addition of NCDs within a specific range decreased the charge transfer resistance (Rct), while excessive NCDs addition actually resulted in reduced conductivity, possibly due to dense nanosheets aggregation, limiting active sites and hindering electrolyte-electrocatalyst interaction. Notably, the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst exhibited the smallest Rct among the electrocatalysts, indicating enhanced conductivity and accelerated reaction kinetics. These findings underscored that the integration of NCDs could modulate the electrocatalytic kinetics of the CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts and enhance their intrinsic electrocatalytic activity.

Furthermore, the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst not only demonstrated superior catalytic activity but also exhibited excellent electrochemical stability. Durability was evaluated at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 by chronoamperometry, revealing that even after 100 h of testing, the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst only showed a slight decrease (Fig. 9 a). Additionally, negligible decline in catalytic performance was observed following 1000 cyclic voltammetry (CV) cycles, with the overpotential increasing by only 4 mV (Fig. 9b). These findings indicated that the incorporation of NCDs enhanced the corrosion resistance of CoHS.

To validate the durability during extended use, the morphology and microstructure of the tested CoHS@NCDs-3 were analyzed. After long-term stability test, compared with the nanoflower structure of untested catalyst (Fig. S4a, b), the tested catalyst overall still adhered to the nickel foam and maintained the nanoflower structure (Fig. S4c), which proved that CoHS@NCDs-3 had good structural stability. And there was also slight dissolution on the surface of the nanoflowers, with small fragments presented (Fig. S4d), we hypothesize that OH– in NCDs may be combined with cobalt ions through coupling, causing the nanosheets to gradually dissolve. After stability testing, the XPS analysis results showed that three peaks of C1s, O1s, and Co2p shifted to lower binding energies (Fig. S5a, b, d). In the O1s spectrum, the OH peak disappears and the Co-O bond strength increases, due to the formation of M * O − by OH (Fig. S5b). The intensity of the high-resolution N spectrum significantly decreased, possibly due to Co-N bond cleavage, indicating that N-containing groups played a major role in the catalytic process (Fig. S5c). A peak of 780 eV was observed in the Co2p spectrum, implying that Co2+evolved into high valence state of cobalt [42]. XPS results evidenced the changes in chemical structures of the catalyst, before and after the testing, due to the OH– of NCDs coupling with cobalt ions. In summary, during the lengthy testing process, there were slight changes in morphology and microstructure, which might be the reason for the performance degradation. But performance of the catalyst still maintained 93 %, therefore CoHS@NCDs-3 possessing excellent durability.

To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the relationship between NCDs and OER performance, a thorough analysis was conducted based on the classic Krasil'schikov pathway for driving OER in alkaline environments [43]. The Tafel slopes of CoHS and the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst were determined to be 117.5 mV dec−1 and 57.1 mV dec−1, respectively (Fig. 7c). Clearly, the rate-determining step (RDS) of CoHS corresponded to a step involving the first electron transfer, while the RDS of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst implicated a subsequent chemical transformation post-reaction. The previous theoretical researches suggested that the improvement of OER performance was not only due to the increase in active centers, but also the enhancement of Co-O covalency and changes in anionic structure, which had a positive impact on intrinsic activity and optimized OER kinetics [44,45]. So in our study, the role of NCDs coupling in facilitating the conversion of M*OH to M*O- on the surface of active sites, thereby optimizing OER kinetics.

To sum up, the CoHS@NCDs composite exhibited excellent OER performance, for several reasons. Firstly, by reasonable controlling the inlet mass of NCDs, the morphology of CoHS@NCDs was regulated to form a three-dimensional flower like structure with a large electrochemical active surface area, thereby increasing the density of active sites. Secondly, there were abundant functional groups on the surface of NCDs, which form a strong association between NCDs and CoHS, providing a large number of channels to promote charge transfer from NCDs to CoHS. Thirdly, small-sized of NCDs exhibited electron donor/acceptor property, increasing the conductivity of CoHS. Finally, the newly generated Co-N and modified cobalt active sites in CoHS@NCDs, served as active centers, optimizing the OER kinetics, enhancing intrinsic activity, and promoting the reaction.

In summary, we performed a comprehensive exploration into the synthesis, characterization, and electrochemical behavior of CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts for OER applications. Initially, NCDs were synthesized using a bottom-up approach and meticulously characterized. Subsequently, the formation of all six CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts was examined, culminating in the identification of the optimal structure of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst. Further analysis confirmed the successful synthesis and composition of the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst. The catalytic performance of the CoHS@NCDs electrocatalysts was then evaluated, with the CoHS@NCDs-3 electrocatalyst exhibiting excellent OER activity, enhanced conductivity, exceptional electrochemical stability, and accelerated reaction kinetics. Overall, the integration of NCDs into CoHS significantly enhanced the catalytic performance and electrochemical durability, offering promising prospects for their application in renewable energy conversion technologies.

Yang Gao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Haiyan Qi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tao Jing: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Jun Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Siqi Shen: Software. Qingxin Zeng: Software. Hongxu Zhao: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (145309525); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2024.118858.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The future of hydrogen energy: Bio-hydrogen production technology
